Hello, no, this is not a heart attack. This can be a cardiac arrhythmia, such as paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or atrial fibrillation, in which the heart rate suddenly goes up and also suddenly decreases. Blood pressure falls during these episodes, and one may have chest pain, dizziness, fainting, blackouts, and sometimes syncope. Usually, the electrocardiogram is normal, as it is conducted between the episodes. An electrocardiogram done during episodes will show abnormalities. You will need twenty-four-hour Holter monitoring if you are having episodes on a daily basis; otherwise, you should rush to the hospital or at least measure your pulse rate during the episode. So, visit a nearby cardiologist for these investigations. Also, hemoglobin and thyroid tests should be done. In order to abort such episodes, you should try to cough hard. Is there any stress or anxiety associated? Until your evaluation is complete, you can ask your local doctor for tablet Clopidrogrel twenty milligrams once a day. If none of the above is successful, then the last option is electrophysiological studies, which is an invasive test that can diagnose and treat the arrhythmia permanently in most cases.

Hello. Before going into the progression of scoliosis, I would like to clarify some facts about it. one) The Cobb angle is a measure of the curvature of the spine in degrees. This helps to determine what type of treatment is necessary. A Cobb angle of ten degrees is regarded as the minimum angulation to define scoliosis. two) A scoliosis curve of ten to fifteen degrees normally does not require any treatment except for regular check-ups. three) If the scoliosis curve is between twenty and forty degrees, the orthopedic doctor will prescribe a back brace to help prevent the spine from developing further curvature. four) Curves greater than forty-five degrees require corrective surgery, which involves the use of pedicle screws and osteotomy to correct the curve. I hope you get an X-ray done of your spine, and it will be easy to measure the curvature properly.

A burning sensation in the urinary tract or urethra can most commonly be due to either an infection of the bladder or urethra, or any stone problem, which may have passed in the bladder-kidney system causing infection and thus burning. It can also be due to unprotected sexual intercourse, which leads to an infection of the urethra. The diagnosis of this condition is important before treatment is initiated. Urine microscopy and sonography of the abdomen and pelvis are initial urine and scanning modalities that are required for diagnosis. A physical examination by a urologist is also of paramount importance as it is often the best modality to establish the diagnosis. Therefore, I would advise you to take plenty of fluids for now, as repeated urination can help wash out the infection. However, the aforementioned tests and physical examination are mandatory in your case. Hepatitis A causes inflammation and scarring of the liver, which causes bile produced not to exit the liver, leading it to spill into the blood, which can be seen as elevated bilirubin levels. To rule out whether there is chronic or acute hepatitis, you can get Immunoglobulin M and Immunoglobulin G Hepatitis A Virus tested. Other tests like Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography can be conducted to rule out obstructive diseases in the bile duct that may have occurred along with the infection. If there is only an infection, then management is symptomatic, allowing time for the liver to heal itself so that gradually the bilirubin levels will subside. All the best.

Tachycardia, also called tachyarrhythmia, is a heart rate that exceeds the normal resting rate. In general, a resting heart rate over one hundred beats per minute is accepted as tachycardia in adults. A heart rate of one hundred and twenty to one hundred and sixty falls within the range of tachycardia. Tachycardia can be part of the body's normal response to anxiety, fever, rapid blood loss, or strenuous exercise. In your case, high anxiety levels are responsible for the tachycardia. Now let's discuss the effect of hormones on tachycardia. Just before the menstrual cycle, there is a change in estrogen and progesterone levels in the body, which has a direct effect. I read your question carefully, and your concern is understandable. So your friend needs to have more tests for some other type of involvement, either a brain issue, which should be revealed through a brain magnetic resonance imaging, or a peripheral nerve issue involving the cranial nerves. Hello. Before discussing the progression of scoliosis, I would like to clarify some facts about it. one) The Cobb angle is a measure of the curvature of the spine in degrees. This helps to determine what type of treatment is necessary. A Cobb angle of ten degrees is regarded as the minimum angulation to define scoliosis. two) A scoliosis curve of ten to fifteen degrees normally does not require any treatment except for regular check-ups. three) If the scoliosis curve is twenty to forty degrees, the orthopedic doctor will prescribe a back brace to help keep the spine from developing more of a curve. four) Curves greater than forty-five degrees require corrective surgery involving many pedicle screws and osteotomies to correct the curvature. I hope you have an X-ray done of your spine, as it will be easy to measure the curvature properly.
